# Transcrição: 01 Lesson #5 Campaigns, Ad Sets & Ads

- **Fonte:** 01 Lesson #5 Campaigns, Ad Sets & Ads
- **Duração:** 13:16
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Hey guys, so in this video what I'm going to talk about is the way that Facebook organizes campaigns. So if you're brand new to Facebook ads, essentially there are three levels within a campaign. So you have what we call the campaign level, the ad set level, and the ad level, which is the actual ads and where you choose your image and the copy and the creative. So let's run through each one of these together now. So as you can see, I'm in ads manager right now and this is where you'll be when you're first logged in, which is what we call the campaign level. So here you'll see all the campaigns that you've got running and they'll be ordered in terms of delivery. So the active ones will show at the top of the inactive campaigns showing towards the bottom. So now what I'm going to do is I'm going to create and explain to you exactly what the campaign level does and especially so that you can understand how it relates to the ad set level. So let's go ahead and click that here. So essentially here are the three levels that I was just telling you about. The campaign, the ad set, and the ad. So Facebook actually tells you what the difference is at the campaign level. So here is what we call the objective. So you have three different categories of objectives, awareness, consideration, and conversion. And this is where we select the objective of our campaign and these are the objectives that you can choose from. So essentially what we're looking at here is, is it a link click campaign? Is it an engagement campaign to get engagement on posts or ads? Is it a video views campaign? Or are we trying to generate leads with the lead generation campaign here? Or is it a conversion campaign? So trying to track and optimize for people buying or people subscribing or people opting into a webinar? Or is it a product catalog sales ad, which is also applicable for hotels or auto car sales, things like that. But that's a bit more advanced. So that'll be covered at a later video in the course. So essentially all you need to know right now is what the objective is that you have for your campaign. So for each campaign, you can only have one objective. So to give you some context, so let's say you wanted an ad to get people to register for a webinar, but you also wanted an ad to get video views. You'd have to do that in two separate campaigns since they're two different objectives. So after the campaign level, we have the ad set level. So I'm just going to go back into ads manager. And I'll show you what it looks like. The ad set level is really where we set the budget, where we set the placement. So is it going to show on mobile? Is it going to show on desktop? Is it going to show on Instagram or audience network? And it's also where we select our targeting. So you might have one campaign, but you want to test out, for example, Australia versus the United States. In order to do that, what you're going to do is create two separate ad sets within the same campaign.

## [03:02 - 06:03]

And one of those ad sets is going to have Australia and one of them is going to have United States, or one might have males and one might have females. It just depends on how you want to separate those. So then what we have is the ad level. And that's really where we're selecting the format of the ad. Is it a video? Is it a carousel? Is it a canvas? Is it one image? And also the text, the copy and the media that we're using. So let's say that we're going to run a conversion campaign. And we'll just leave that as the default name there. And now you'll see that we're in the ad set level. So this is where we're going to select the conversion that we want to track for in here, whether it's view content or search, or it should add to cart or add to basket. It just depends on what language you're set to or whatever event that you want to optimize for. So you can see none of these have ever been triggered before because this is a dummy account. But for you guys, if the event is available to be used, that'll show as green for ready instead of these red ones that I've got here. So we'll just pick one of these for now. So you're going to select the conversion that you want to track for. And then over here is where you're going to select your targeting. So are we using a lookalike audience? So these are done under the audiences tab over here, or you can create a lookalike straight from here. Or are we using a custom audience? So people who have visited your website or an email list or a video views audience or whatever that might be. So this is where you can select that as well. And you can also choose the locations, age, gender, languages, any interests and things like that. But we're going to cover all of that in a later video. This is just to give you an overview of what the ad set level is about. Then what we have here is placements. Now be super careful about this one because by default Facebook will always choose automatic placements. But the thing is if you're running a link click campaign or a page post or video views or even a conversion campaign, Facebook is more than likely going to feed your ad into audience network and mobile a lot more than it's going to feed it into the news feed and the desktop news feed. So be really careful here because you want to sort of control this and make sure that you're selecting the placements that you actually want in your ad set. So what you could do in your campaigns is test between mobile and desktop. So you'd have two different ad sets that use the same targeting but they'll be testing mobile and one and desktop and the other. Most of the time I'd avoid audience network unless it's for an app. Audience network down here is third party placements. So your ad is being shown in games and in apps and it tends to get lots and lots of cheap clicks, but they don't typically tend to be high quality clicks. So just be really wary of using this one. So then down here you can select your budget and you can select daily or lifetime.

## [06:03 - 09:09]

You can run it continuously or set a start and end date for it. That's all up to you. And if you scroll down here, you can also select your optimization. So conversions or landing page views, link clicks, impressions or daily unique reach. And Facebook gives you a description of what each of those are over here as well. After that you have your conversion window down here. So this is based on how soon after clicking or viewing your ad do people typically convert on your website. So default here is seven days after clicking or one day after viewing. So we'll just leave it as that for now. And then you have your bidding strategy, whether it's going to be automatic or manual. And again, Facebook gives you some idea of what those are here. When you're charged, which is set to impressions, your ad scheduling, which is only available for lifetime budget campaigns. Your delivery type, standard or accelerated accelerated only works with manual bid. And then after that we will click continue. And if you want to name your ad set, you would do that up here. So you can put any descriptors in there in terms of the placements that you're using or who you're targeting. Cool. So we will just click continue. So after we press continue, you can see that we're now at the ad level over here. So this is where we choose the format, whether it's going to be a carousel ad, single image, single video, slideshow or collection. And if we wanted to create a canvas ad, then we would toggle this add a full screen experience down here. Then as you come down here, we will just click a single image for now. You're going to add in your image. So you can use a stock image from Facebook here, whatever that is, add in your URL. Your text, your headline, your call to action. And make sure your pixels tracking. And then click confirm to place the order for that ad. So that's a bit of an idea of how everything fits together. So now we're back in ads manager and you can see here that we've got two campaigns in here. At the moment we have one called video views and one called conversions. So one of our objectives is to get more video views on a video. And then for the second one, we want to get people to buy a product. So we're retargeting people that visited the website or who have viewed the video in here. Now we've separated these into two separate campaigns because there are essentially two different objectives. So remember the campaign level is where we choose the objectives for our campaigns. So if we go into this video views campaign here, what you'll see is that we've got three different ad sets set up. So all of them are targeting the United States. And then what you'll see is that two of these are targeting the interest snorkeling, one on desktop and one on mobile.

## [09:09 - 12:10]

And then we've got one targeting the interest GoPro and this one's just on mobile. The reason why we've separated these is different ad sets is because we're trying to test out the targeting. So with the interest targeting, we want to see which interest targeting or which interest selection is going to work best. But we separate our desktop and mobile because if we didn't do this, if we put them under the same ad set, then Facebook's going to feed it mostly into mobile because that's where the real estate is. So that's why we have to separate those out. And some clients will convert better on desktop and some will convert better on mobile and there's no rhyme or reason between it really. So it's always a good idea to test that. So then if we go down to the ad level, we'll just choose one of these. You'll see, I think there's just one ad and each ad set at the moment. So something to note here is that what tends to happen a lot is that a lot of marketers will run five or six or even 10 new images within the same ad set when they're initially testing. So they're going to have lots and lots of images that they're going to split test. Now, the problem with that is that Facebook will pick sides for the ones that it prefers, but they're not necessarily going to be the ones that will work best and it will favor those ads and give them all of the spend and impressions. But meanwhile, those may not actually be the best ads and so therefore it might not be working in your favor. So if you do want to split test lots and lots of different images or copy variations, we recommend that you create a different ad set to do that. So you can do up to three in each ad set. So what you would do then is go back to the ad set level and you're going to duplicate your ad set. So you can click it up here or you can click it right here. You make sure that's going in the original campaign and you can choose how many copies of the ad set if you want. So if you wanted to do nine different creatives, you could duplicate that twice and then you'd have three ad sets with three ads in it and you click the duplicate button. And then we'll just leave this as a draft now and go to the ads level and you'll just come in here and edit your ad. So you can change out the image or you can change out the copy in here and if you haven't created your duplicates yet, you can duplicate your ads as well so you can make all of your variations in there. But don't do more than three under each ad set so that you can probably test your ads. If you have more than three, then Facebook is going to optimize it within the first few hours and it doesn't really work very well and you're not going to get a good picture of what Facebook prefers or what's going to work best for your audience. So that's essentially how campaigns relate to ad sets and how ad sets relate to ads. The ad set is what allows you to test your different targeting and bidding strategies and placements and the ads obviously that's your creative and your campaign level is where you're setting your objective. So the way that we recommend that you organize your campaigns is to essentially look at your funnel strategy and your funnel setup that you have going for your client.

## [12:10 - 13:16]

So say you have a video at the top of funnel which you're going to use video views for as a campaign objective and then in the middle of funnel you have a link click campaign to send people to the website and then you're following that with some website retargeting as well. So there's really three steps in your funnel. So what you're going to do is essentially have three campaigns because you just divide your campaigns by the steps in your funnel and then if you're wanting to test between video views and a link click at the top of funnel though then that would involve creating two separate campaigns because those are two different objectives. But essentially typically if you have a three step funnel and you're very clear on those three steps and what's going to work then you should only have three campaigns. And then with underneath each of these campaigns you're going to have lots and lots of different ad sets testing your different fitting strategies or different targeting options or placements and then at the ad level you're going to be testing your different creatives and different headlines and everything like that. So I hope this all makes sense guys and again if you have any questions whatsoever feel free to post them in the group.
